* Office hours (entry possible) are detailed on the official website == History == *1877 (Meiji 10) - A spirit invocation ceremony for war dead soldiers of the government forces was held at {{ill|Shizuoka Sengen Shrine|ja|静岡浅間神社}} (Kokuhen-sha, Sōsha of Suruga), and ceremonies continued at a corner of the shrine buildings. *Individual spirit invocation ceremonies for war dead were held in the three regions of Suruga, Izu, and Hamamatsu. *In the late 1880s, there was a growing movement to construct a single spirit invocation shrine for the entire prefecture. *November 13, 1899 (Meiji 32) - founded as a "Shared Spirit Invocation Shrine" with joint public and private support, in Kitabancho, Shizuoka City. *April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" by designation of the Ministry of the Interior. *October 8, 1942 (Shōwa 17) - Relocated to the current site due to the narrowness of the old site in central Shizuoka City. *March 28, 1946 (Shōwa 21) - established as a religious corporation under the Religious Corporations Act. *1947 (Shōwa 22) - Renamed "Shizutama Shrine" considering post-war conditions. *December 1952 (Shōwa 27) - Registered as a religious corporation with the approval of the prefectural governor. *1953 (Shōwa 28) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" and added to the list of Bekkaku Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. *1978 (Shōwa 53) - New shrine office built due to the aging and narrowness of the old office. *October 22-23, 1998 (Heisei 10) - Centenary commemorative festival held. *October 22-23, 2018 (Heisei 30) - 120th anniversary commemorative festival held.
== Major festivals == [[File:Shizuoka ken Senbotsu Sensaishisha Ireihyo 19521103.jpg|thumb|200px|Memorial monument for the war dead and war victims of Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Designed by Tadashi Tanaka Architecture Office, constructed by Hirai Industry. 'Memorial Monument for the War Dead and War Victims of Shizuoka Prefecture', 1952.</ref>]] * Saitansai - January 1 * Genshisai - January 3 * Setsubunsai - February 3 * Kigensai - February 11 * Kinensai - February 17 * Tenchousai - February 23 * Shizuoka Prefecture Army War Dead Memorial Service - March 10 * Shōwasai - April 29 * Shōbu festival - May 5 * Shizuoka Prefecture Navy War Dead Memorial Service - May 27 * Otaue festival (at Shinsen-den) - June (auspicious day) * Nagoshi Oharae ceremony (Chinowa Kuguri) - June 30 * Tanabata festival - Early July (auspicious day) * Mantō Mitama festival - August 13-15 * Eirei Kenshōsai - August 13-15 * Nukihosai (at Shinsen-den) - October (auspicious day) * Harvest Thanksgiving festival - Same day as Nukihosai * Reiji Hoan ceremony (Gōshisai) - October 21 (when applicable) * festival "Irei Taisai" - October 22-23 * Meijisai - November 3 * Foundation Memorial festival - November 13 * Shichi-Go-San festival - November 15 * Niinamesai - November 23 * Oosusuharai festival - December 13 * Year-End Oharae ceremony - December 31 * New Year's Eve festival - December 31 * Tsukishizusai - Monthly on the 1st (except January, when Saitansai is held) * Tsukizusai - Monthly on the 13th (except November, when the Foundation Memorial festival is held) * Memorial Day ceremony - Several times monthly, and on memorial anniversaries as needed * Individual Unit Memorial Services - Held regularly (refer to official website)

The shrine crest is a circle with cherry blossoms. It enshrines over 76,000 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture from the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} to the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}.
During the war, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of the Interior (with prefectural rank). After the separation of state and shrine post-war, it was established and registered as a religious corporation under the newly formed {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. On May 29, 1953, it was added to the list of Bekkaku Shrines. The war relics hall on the second floor of the shrine office was relocated to the newly constructed Tsutsuji Hall, which also houses the Shizuoka Prefecture Bereaved Families Association Office, displaying about 1,000 war relics and portraits of the war dead.

'''Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine''' (静岡縣護國神社[[ja:静岡縣護國神社]], Shizuoka-ken Gokoku Jinja) is a shrine located in {{ill|Shizuoka City|ja|静岡市}}, {{ill|Aoi Ward, Shizuoka|ja|葵区}}. The shrine crest is a circle with cherry blossoms. It enshrines over 76,000 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture from the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} to the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}.
During the war, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of the Interior (with prefectural rank). After the separation of state and shrine post-war, it was established and registered as a religious corporation under the newly formed {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. On May 29, 1953, it was added to the list of Bekkaku Shrines. The war relics hall on the second floor of the shrine office was relocated to the newly constructed Tsutsuji Hall, which also houses the Shizuoka Prefecture Bereaved Families Association Office, displaying about 1,000 war relics and portraits of the war dead.
== Hours == General worship hours: Shrine grounds are open from 6 AM to 5 PM (6 PM in spring and summer) * After closing hours, worship is allowed from a distance ≒ 24 hours operation (due to business profile limitations, this notation is used)
* Office hours (entry possible) are detailed on the official website == History == *1877 (Meiji 10) - A spirit invocation ceremony for war dead soldiers of the government forces was held at {{ill|Shizuoka Sengen Shrine|ja|静岡浅間神社}} (Kokuhen-sha, Sōsha of Suruga), and ceremonies continued at a corner of the shrine buildings. *Individual spirit invocation ceremonies for war dead were held in the three regions of Suruga, Izu, and Hamamatsu. *In the late 1880s, there was a growing movement to construct a single spirit invocation shrine for the entire prefecture. *November 13, 1899 (Meiji 32) - founded as a "Shared Spirit Invocation Shrine" with joint public and private support, in Kitabancho, Shizuoka City. *April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" by designation of the Ministry of the Interior. *October 8, 1942 (Shōwa 17) - Relocated to the current site due to the narrowness of the old site in central Shizuoka City. *March 28, 1946 (Shōwa 21) - established as a religious corporation under the Religious Corporations Act. *1947 (Shōwa 22) - Renamed "Shizutama Shrine" considering post-war conditions. *December 1952 (Shōwa 27) - Registered as a religious corporation with the approval of the prefectural governor. *1953 (Shōwa 28) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" and added to the list of Bekkaku Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. *1978 (Shōwa 53) - New shrine office built due to the aging and narrowness of the old office. *October 22-23, 1998 (Heisei 10) - Centenary commemorative festival held. *October 22-23, 2018 (Heisei 30) - 120th anniversary commemorative festival held.
== Major festivals == [[File:Shizuoka ken Senbotsu Sensaishisha Ireihyo 19521103.jpg|thumb|200px|Memorial monument for the war dead and war victims of Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Designed by Tadashi Tanaka Architecture Office, constructed by Hirai Industry. 'Memorial Monument for the War Dead and War Victims of Shizuoka Prefecture', 1952.</ref>]] * Saitansai - January 1 * Genshisai - January 3 * Setsubunsai - February 3 * Kigensai - February 11 * Kinensai - February 17 * Tenchousai - February 23 * Shizuoka Prefecture Army War Dead Memorial Service - March 10 * Shōwasai - April 29 * Shōbu festival - May 5 * Shizuoka Prefecture Navy War Dead Memorial Service - May 27 * Otaue festival (at Shinsen-den) - June (auspicious day) * Nagoshi Oharae ceremony (Chinowa Kuguri) - June 30 * Tanabata festival - Early July (auspicious day) * Mantō Mitama festival - August 13-15 * Eirei Kenshōsai - August 13-15 * Nukihosai (at Shinsen-den) - October (auspicious day) * Harvest Thanksgiving festival - Same day as Nukihosai * Reiji Hoan ceremony (Gōshisai) - October 21 (when applicable) * festival "Irei Taisai" - October 22-23 * Meijisai - November 3 * Foundation Memorial festival - November 13 * Shichi-Go-San festival - November 15 * Niinamesai - November 23 * Oosusuharai festival - December 13 * Year-End Oharae ceremony - December 31 * New Year's Eve festival - December 31 * Tsukishizusai - Monthly on the 1st (except January, when Saitansai is held) * Tsukizusai - Monthly on the 13th (except November, when the Foundation Memorial festival is held) * Memorial Day ceremony - Several times monthly, and on memorial anniversaries as needed * Individual Unit Memorial Services - Held regularly (refer to official website)

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:04 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine |image=[[File:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine.jpg|250px|Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]] |location=Shizuoka Prefecture, Shizuoka City, Aoi Ward, Yuzuki 366 |deities=76,233 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture |rank=Interior Minister Designated Gokoku Shrine, Bekkaku Shrine |founded=1899 (Meiji 32) |main_hall=Nagare-zukuri |annual_festival={{ill|October 22|ja|10月22日}}, {{ill|October 23|ja|10月23日}} |latitude = 34.9875 |longitude = 138.4070 |ISO = JP-22 |label_position = bottom |map= Japan Shizuoka }} [[ja:靜岡縣護國神社]]
'''Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine''' (静岡縣護國神社[[ja:静岡縣護國神社]], Shizuoka-ken Gokoku Jinja) is a shrine located in {{ill|Shizuoka City|ja|静岡市}}, {{ill|Aoi Ward, Shizuoka|ja|葵区}}. The shrine crest is a circle with cherry blossoms. It enshrines over 76,000 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture from the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} to the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}.
During the war, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of the Interior (with prefectural rank). After the separation of state and shrine post-war, it was established and registered as a religious corporation under the newly formed {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. On May 29, 1953, it was added to the list of Bekkaku Shrines. The war relics hall on the second floor of the shrine office was relocated to the newly constructed Tsutsuji Hall, which also houses the Shizuoka Prefecture Bereaved Families Association Office, displaying about 1,000 war relics and portraits of the war dead.
== Hours == General worship hours: Shrine grounds are open from 6 AM to 5 PM (6 PM in spring and summer) * After closing hours, worship is allowed from a distance ≒ 24 hours operation (due to business profile limitations, this notation is used)
* Office hours (entry possible) are detailed on the official website == History == *1877 (Meiji 10) - A spirit invocation ceremony for war dead soldiers of the government forces was held at {{ill|Shizuoka Sengen Shrine|ja|静岡浅間神社}} (Kokuhen-sha, Sōsha of Suruga), and ceremonies continued at a corner of the shrine buildings. *Individual spirit invocation ceremonies for war dead were held in the three regions of Suruga, Izu, and Hamamatsu. *In the late 1880s, there was a growing movement to construct a single spirit invocation shrine for the entire prefecture. *November 13, 1899 (Meiji 32) - founded as a "Shared Spirit Invocation Shrine" with joint public and private support, in Kitabancho, Shizuoka City. *April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" by designation of the Ministry of the Interior. *October 8, 1942 (Shōwa 17) - Relocated to the current site due to the narrowness of the old site in central Shizuoka City. *March 28, 1946 (Shōwa 21) - established as a religious corporation under the Religious Corporations Act. *1947 (Shōwa 22) - Renamed "Shizutama Shrine" considering post-war conditions. *December 1952 (Shōwa 27) - Registered as a religious corporation with the approval of the prefectural governor. *1953 (Shōwa 28) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" and added to the list of Bekkaku Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. *1978 (Shōwa 53) - New shrine office built due to the aging and narrowness of the old office. *October 22-23, 1998 (Heisei 10) - Centenary commemorative festival held. *October 22-23, 2018 (Heisei 30) - 120th anniversary commemorative festival held.
== Major festivals == [[File:Shizuoka ken Senbotsu Sensaishisha Ireihyo 19521103.jpg|thumb|200px|Memorial monument for the war dead and war victims of Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Designed by Tadashi Tanaka Architecture Office, constructed by Hirai Industry. 'Memorial Monument for the War Dead and War Victims of Shizuoka Prefecture', 1952.</ref>]] * Saitansai - January 1 * Genshisai - January 3 * Setsubunsai - February 3 * Kigensai - February 11 * Kinensai - February 17 * Tenchousai - February 23 * Shizuoka Prefecture Army War Dead Memorial Service - March 10 * Shōwasai - April 29 * Shōbu festival - May 5 * Shizuoka Prefecture Navy War Dead Memorial Service - May 27 * Otaue festival (at Shinsen-den) - June (auspicious day) * Nagoshi Oharae ceremony (Chinowa Kuguri) - June 30 * Tanabata festival - Early July (auspicious day) * Mantō Mitama festival - August 13-15 * Eirei Kenshōsai - August 13-15 * Nukihosai (at Shinsen-den) - October (auspicious day) * Harvest Thanksgiving festival - Same day as Nukihosai * Reiji Hoan ceremony (Gōshisai) - October 21 (when applicable) * festival "Irei Taisai" - October 22-23 * Meijisai - November 3 * Foundation Memorial festival - November 13 * Shichi-Go-San festival - November 15 * Niinamesai - November 23 * Oosusuharai festival - December 13 * Year-End Oharae ceremony - December 31 * New Year's Eve festival - December 31 * Tsukishizusai - Monthly on the 1st (except January, when Saitansai is held) * Tsukizusai - Monthly on the 13th (except November, when the Foundation Memorial festival is held) * Memorial Day ceremony - Several times monthly, and on memorial anniversaries as needed * Individual Unit Memorial Services - Held regularly (refer to official website)
== References == {{Reflist}}

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:04 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine |image=[[File:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine.jpg|250px|Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]] |location=Shizuoka Prefecture, Shizuoka City, Aoi Ward, Yuzuki 366 |deities=76,233 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture |rank=Interior Minister Designated Gokoku Shrine, Bekkaku Shrine |founded=1899 (Meiji 32) |main_hall=Nagare-zukuri |annual_festival={{ill|October 22|ja|10月22日}}, {{ill|October 23|ja|10月23日}} |latitude = 34.9875 |longitude = 138.4070 |ISO = JP-22 |label_position = bottom |map= Japan Shizuoka }} [[ja:靜岡縣護國神社]]
'''Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine''' (静岡縣護國神社[[ja:静岡縣護國神社]], Shizuoka-ken Gokoku Jinja) is a shrine located in {{ill|Shizuoka City|ja|静岡市}}, {{ill|Aoi Ward, Shizuoka|ja|葵区}}. The shrine crest is a circle with cherry blossoms. It enshrines over 76,000 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture from the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} to the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}.
During the war, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of the Interior (with prefectural rank). After the separation of state and shrine post-war, it was established and registered as a religious corporation under the newly formed {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. On May 29, 1953, it was added to the list of Bekkaku Shrines. The war relics hall on the second floor of the shrine office was relocated to the newly constructed Tsutsuji Hall, which also houses the Shizuoka Prefecture Bereaved Families Association Office, displaying about 1,000 war relics and portraits of the war dead.
== Hours == General worship hours: Shrine grounds are open from 6 AM to 5 PM (6 PM in spring and summer) * After closing hours, worship is allowed from a distance ≒ 24 hours operation (due to business profile limitations, this notation is used)
* Office hours (entry possible) are detailed on the official website == History == *1877 (Meiji 10) - A spirit invocation ceremony for war dead soldiers of the government forces was held at {{ill|Shizuoka Sengen Shrine|ja|静岡浅間神社}} (Kokuhen-sha, Sōsha of Suruga), and ceremonies continued at a corner of the shrine buildings. *Individual spirit invocation ceremonies for war dead were held in the three regions of Suruga, Izu, and Hamamatsu. *In the late 1880s, there was a growing movement to construct a single spirit invocation shrine for the entire prefecture. *November 13, 1899 (Meiji 32) - founded as a "Shared Spirit Invocation Shrine" with joint public and private support, in Kitabancho, Shizuoka City. *April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" by designation of the Ministry of the Interior. *October 8, 1942 (Shōwa 17) - Relocated to the current site due to the narrowness of the old site in central Shizuoka City. *March 28, 1946 (Shōwa 21) - established as a religious corporation under the Religious Corporations Act. *1947 (Shōwa 22) - Renamed "Shizutama Shrine" considering post-war conditions. *December 1952 (Shōwa 27) - Registered as a religious corporation with the approval of the prefectural governor. *1953 (Shōwa 28) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" and added to the list of Bekkaku Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. *1978 (Shōwa 53) - New shrine office built due to the aging and narrowness of the old office. *October 22-23, 1998 (Heisei 10) - Centenary commemorative festival held. *October 22-23, 2018 (Heisei 30) - 120th anniversary commemorative festival held.
== Major festivals == [[File:Shizuoka ken Senbotsu Sensaishisha Ireihyo 19521103.jpg|thumb|200px|Memorial monument for the war dead and war victims of Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Designed by Tadashi Tanaka Architecture Office, constructed by Hirai Industry. 'Memorial Monument for the War Dead and War Victims of Shizuoka Prefecture', 1952.</ref>]] * Saitansai - January 1 * Genshisai - January 3 * Setsubunsai - February 3 * Kigensai - February 11 * Kinensai - February 17 * Tenchousai - February 23 * Shizuoka Prefecture Army War Dead Memorial Service - March 10 * Shōwasai - April 29 * Shōbu festival - May 5 * Shizuoka Prefecture Navy War Dead Memorial Service - May 27 * Otaue festival (at Shinsen-den) - June (auspicious day) * Nagoshi Oharae ceremony (Chinowa Kuguri) - June 30 * Tanabata festival - Early July (auspicious day) * Mantō Mitama festival - August 13-15 * Eirei Kenshōsai - August 13-15 * Nukihosai (at Shinsen-den) - October (auspicious day) * Harvest Thanksgiving festival - Same day as Nukihosai * Reiji Hoan ceremony (Gōshisai) - October 21 (when applicable) * festival "Irei Taisai" - October 22-23 * Meijisai - November 3 * Foundation Memorial festival - November 13 * Shichi-Go-San festival - November 15 * Niinamesai - November 23 * Oosusuharai festival - December 13 * Year-End Oharae ceremony - December 31 * New Year's Eve festival - December 31 * Tsukishizusai - Monthly on the 1st (except January, when Saitansai is held) * Tsukizusai - Monthly on the 13th (except November, when the Foundation Memorial festival is held) * Memorial Day ceremony - Several times monthly, and on memorial anniversaries as needed * Individual Unit Memorial Services - Held regularly (refer to official website)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Shizuoka Army Cemetery|ja|静岡陸軍墓地}} [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:pages needing attribution]] == External Links == * [https://shizuokagokoku.jp/ Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine (Official Site)] * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4406003 Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine (Shizuoka Prefecture Shrine Office)] {{Shinto-navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}}

After the separation of state and shrine post-war, it was established and registered as a religious corporation under the newly formed {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. On May 29, 1953, it was added to the list of Bekkaku Shrines. The war relics hall on the second floor of the shrine office was relocated to the newly constructed Tsutsuji Hall, which also houses the Shizuoka Prefecture Bereaved Families Association Office, displaying about 1,000 war relics and portraits of the war dead.
== Hours == General worship hours: Shrine grounds are open from 6 AM to 5 PM (6 PM in spring and summer) * After closing hours, worship is allowed from a distance ≒ 24 hours operation (due to business profile limitations, this notation is used)
* Office hours (entry possible) are detailed on the official website == History == *1877 (Meiji 10) - A spirit invocation ceremony for war dead soldiers of the government forces was held at {{ill|Shizuoka Sengen Shrine|ja|静岡浅間神社}} (Kokuhen-sha, Sōsha of Suruga), and ceremonies continued at a corner of the shrine buildings. *Individual spirit invocation ceremonies for war dead were held in the three regions of Suruga, Izu, and Hamamatsu. *In the late 1880s, there was a growing movement to construct a single spirit invocation shrine for the entire prefecture. *November 13, 1899 (Meiji 32) - founded as a "Shared Spirit Invocation Shrine" with joint public and private support, in Kitabancho, Shizuoka City. *April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" by designation of the Ministry of the Interior. *October 8, 1942 (Shōwa 17) - Relocated to the current site due to the narrowness of the old site in central Shizuoka City. *March 28, 1946 (Shōwa 21) - established as a religious corporation under the Religious Corporations Act. *1947 (Shōwa 22) - Renamed "Shizutama Shrine" considering post-war conditions. *December 1952 (Shōwa 27) - Registered as a religious corporation with the approval of the prefectural governor. *1953 (Shōwa 28) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" and added to the list of Bekkaku Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. *1978 (Shōwa 53) - New shrine office built due to the aging and narrowness of the old office. *October 22-23, 1998 (Heisei 10) - Centenary commemorative festival held. *October 22-23, 2018 (Heisei 30) - 120th anniversary commemorative festival held.
== Major festivals == [[File:Shizuoka ken Senbotsu Sensaishisha Ireihyo 19521103.jpg|thumb|200px|Memorial monument for the war dead and war victims of Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Designed by Tadashi Tanaka Architecture Office, constructed by Hirai Industry. 'Memorial Monument for the War Dead and War Victims of Shizuoka Prefecture', 1952.</ref>]] * Saitansai - January 1 * Genshisai - January 3 * Setsubunsai - February 3 * Kigensai - February 11 * Kinensai - February 17 * Tenchousai - February 23 * Shizuoka Prefecture Army War Dead Memorial Service - March 10 * Shōwasai - April 29 * Shōbu festival - May 5 * Shizuoka Prefecture Navy War Dead Memorial Service - May 27 * Otaue festival (at Shinsen-den) - June (auspicious day) * Nagoshi Oharae ceremony (Chinowa Kuguri) - June 30 * Tanabata festival - Early July (auspicious day) * Mantō Mitama festival - August 13-15 * Eirei Kenshōsai - August 13-15 * Nukihosai (at Shinsen-den) - October (auspicious day) * Harvest Thanksgiving festival - Same day as Nukihosai * Reiji Hoan ceremony (Gōshisai) - October 21 (when applicable) * festival "Irei Taisai" - October 22-23 * Meijisai - November 3 * Foundation Memorial festival - November 13 * Shichi-Go-San festival - November 15 * Niinamesai - November 23 * Oosusuharai festival - December 13 * Year-End Oharae ceremony - December 31 * New Year's Eve festival - December 31 * Tsukishizusai - Monthly on the 1st (except January, when Saitansai is held) * Tsukizusai - Monthly on the 13th (except November, when the Foundation Memorial festival is held) * Memorial Day ceremony - Several times monthly, and on memorial anniversaries as needed * Individual Unit Memorial Services - Held regularly (refer to official website)

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:04 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine |image=[[File:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine.jpg|250px|Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]] |location=Shizuoka Prefecture, Shizuoka City, Aoi Ward, Yuzuki 366 |deity=76,233 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture |rank=Interior Minister Designated Gokoku Shrine, Bekkaku Shrine |founded=1899 (Meiji 32) |main_hall=Nagare-zukuri |annual_festival={{ill|October 22|ja|10月22日}}, {{ill|October 23|ja|10月23日}} |latitude = 34.9875 |longitude = 138.4070 |ISO = JP-22 |label_position = bottom |map= Japan Shizuoka }} [[ja:靜岡縣護國神社]]
'''Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine''' (静岡縣護國神社[[ja:静岡縣護國神社]], Shizuoka-ken Gokoku Jinja) is a shrine located in {{ill|Shizuoka City|ja|静岡市}}, {{ill|Aoi Ward, Shizuoka|ja|葵区}}. The shrine crest is a circle with cherry blossoms. It enshrines over 76,000 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture from the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} to the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}.
During the war, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of the Interior (with prefectural rank). After the separation of state and shrine post-war, it was established and registered as a religious corporation under the newly formed {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. On May 29, 1953, it was added to the list of Bekkaku Shrines. The war relics hall on the second floor of the shrine office was relocated to the newly constructed Tsutsuji Hall, which also houses the Shizuoka Prefecture Bereaved Families Association Office, displaying about 1,000 war relics and portraits of the war dead.
== Hours == General worship hours: Shrine grounds are open from 6 AM to 5 PM (6 PM in spring and summer) * After closing hours, worship is allowed from a distance ≒ 24 hours operation (due to business profile limitations, this notation is used)
* Office hours (entry possible) are detailed on the official website == History == *1877 (Meiji 10) - A spirit invocation ceremony for war dead soldiers of the government forces was held at {{ill|Shizuoka Sengen Shrine|ja|静岡浅間神社}} (Kokuhen-sha, Sōsha of Suruga), and ceremonies continued at a corner of the shrine buildings. *Individual spirit invocation ceremonies for war dead were held in the three regions of Suruga, Izu, and Hamamatsu. *In the late 1880s, there was a growing movement to construct a single spirit invocation shrine for the entire prefecture. *November 13, 1899 (Meiji 32) - founded as a "Shared Spirit Invocation Shrine" with joint public and private support, in Kitabancho, Shizuoka City. *April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" by designation of the Ministry of the Interior. *October 8, 1942 (Shōwa 17) - Relocated to the current site due to the narrowness of the old site in central Shizuoka City. *March 28, 1946 (Shōwa 21) - established as a religious corporation under the Religious Corporations Act. *1947 (Shōwa 22) - Renamed "Shizutama Shrine" considering post-war conditions. *December 1952 (Shōwa 27) - Registered as a religious corporation with the approval of the prefectural governor. *1953 (Shōwa 28) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" and added to the list of Bekkaku Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. *1978 (Shōwa 53) - New shrine office built due to the aging and narrowness of the old office. *October 22-23, 1998 (Heisei 10) - Centenary commemorative festival held. *October 22-23, 2018 (Heisei 30) - 120th anniversary commemorative festival held.
== Major festivals == [[File:Shizuoka ken Senbotsu Sensaishisha Ireihyo 19521103.jpg|thumb|200px|Memorial monument for the war dead and war victims of Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Designed by Tadashi Tanaka Architecture Office, constructed by Hirai Industry. 'Memorial Monument for the War Dead and War Victims of Shizuoka Prefecture', 1952.</ref>]] * Saitansai - January 1 * Genshisai - January 3 * Setsubunsai - February 3 * Kigensai - February 11 * Kinensai - February 17 * Tenchousai - February 23 * Shizuoka Prefecture Army War Dead Memorial Service - March 10 * Shōwasai - April 29 * Shōbu festival - May 5 * Shizuoka Prefecture Navy War Dead Memorial Service - May 27 * Otaue festival (at Shinsen-den) - June (auspicious day) * Nagoshi Oharae ceremony (Chinowa Kuguri) - June 30 * Tanabata festival - Early July (auspicious day) * Mantō Mitama festival - August 13-15 * Eirei Kenshōsai - August 13-15 * Nukihosai (at Shinsen-den) - October (auspicious day) * Harvest Thanksgiving festival - Same day as Nukihosai * Reiji Hoan ceremony (Gōshisai) - October 21 (when applicable) * festival "Irei Taisai" - October 22-23 * Meijisai - November 3 * Foundation Memorial festival - November 13 * Shichi-Go-San festival - November 15 * Niinamesai - November 23 * Oosusuharai festival - December 13 * Year-End Oharae ceremony - December 31 * New Year's Eve festival - December 31 * Tsukishizusai - Monthly on the 1st (except January, when Saitansai is held) * Tsukizusai - Monthly on the 13th (except November, when the Foundation Memorial festival is held) * Memorial Day ceremony - Several times monthly, and on memorial anniversaries as needed * Individual Unit Memorial Services - Held regularly (refer to official website)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Shizuoka Army Cemetery|ja|静岡陸軍墓地}} [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]]== External Links == * [https://shizuokagokoku.jp/ Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine (Official Site)] * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4406003 Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine (Shizuoka Prefecture Shrine Office)] {{Shinto-navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}}

'Memorial Monument for the War Dead and War Victims of Shizuoka Prefecture', 1952.</ref>]] * Saitansai - January 1 * Genshisai - January 3 * Setsubunsai - February 3 * Kigensai - February 11 * Kinensai - February 17 * Tenchousai - February 23 * Shizuoka Prefecture Army War Dead Memorial Service - March 10 * Shōwasai - April 29 * Shōbu festival - May 5 * Shizuoka Prefecture Navy War Dead Memorial Service - May 27 * Otaue festival (at Shinsen-den) - June (auspicious day) * Nagoshi Oharae ceremony (Chinowa Kuguri) - June 30 * Tanabata festival - Early July (auspicious day) * Mantō Mitama festival - August 13-15 * Eirei Kenshōsai - August 13-15 * Nukihosai (at Shinsen-den) - October (auspicious day) * Harvest Thanksgiving festival - Same day as Nukihosai * Reiji Hoan ceremony (Gōshisai) - October 21 (when applicable) * festival "Irei Taisai" - October 22-23 * Meijisai - November 3 * Foundation Memorial festival - November 13 * Shichi-Go-San festival - November 15 * Niinamesai - November 23 * Oosusuharai festival - December 13 * Year-End Oharae ceremony - December 31 * New Year's Eve festival - December 31 * Tsukishizusai - Monthly on the 1st (except January, when Saitansai is held) * Tsukizusai - Monthly on the 13th (except November, when the Foundation Memorial festival is held) * Memorial Day ceremony - Several times monthly, and on memorial anniversaries as needed * Individual Unit Memorial Services - Held regularly (refer to official website)

The shrine crest is a circle with cherry blossoms. It enshrines over 76,000 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture from the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} to the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}.
During the war, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of the Interior (with prefectural rank). After the separation of state and shrine post-war, it was established and registered as a religious corporation under the newly formed {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. On May 29, 1953, it was added to the list of Bekkaku Shrines. The war relics hall on the second floor of the shrine office was relocated to the newly constructed Tsutsuji Hall, which also houses the Shizuoka Prefecture Bereaved Families Association Office, displaying about 1,000 war relics and portraits of the war dead.

revision.text:
{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:04 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine |image=[[File:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine.jpg|250px|Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]] |location=Shizuoka Prefecture, Shizuoka City, Aoi Ward, Yuzuki 366 |deity=76,233 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture |rank=Interior Minister Designated Gokoku Shrine, Beppyo Shrine |founded=1899 (Meiji 32) |main_hall=Nagare-zukuri |annual_festival={{ill|October 22|ja|10月22日}}, {{ill|October 23|ja|10月23日}} |latitude = 34.9875 |longitude = 138.4070 |ISO = JP-22 |label_position = bottom |map= Japan Shizuoka }} [[ja:靜岡縣護國神社]]
'''Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine''' (静岡縣護國神社[[ja:静岡縣護國神社]], Shizuoka-ken Gokoku Jinja) is a shrine located in {{ill|Shizuoka City|ja|静岡市}}, {{ill|Aoi Ward, Shizuoka|ja|葵区}}. The shrine crest is a circle with cherry blossoms. It enshrines over 76,000 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture from the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} to the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}.
During the war, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of the Interior (with prefectural rank). After the separation of state and shrine post-war, it was established and registered as a religious corporation under the newly formed {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. On May 29, 1953, it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines. The war relics hall on the second floor of the shrine office was relocated to the newly constructed Tsutsuji Hall, which also houses the Shizuoka Prefecture Bereaved Families Association Office, displaying about 1,000 war relics and portraits of the war dead.
== Hours == General worship hours: Shrine grounds are open from 6 AM to 5 PM (6 PM in spring and summer) * After closing hours, worship is allowed from a distance ≒ 24 hours operation (due to business profile limitations, this notation is used)
* Office hours (entry possible) are detailed on the official website == History == *1877 (Meiji 10) - A spirit invocation ceremony for war dead soldiers of the government forces was held at {{ill|Shizuoka Sengen Shrine|ja|静岡浅間神社}} (Kokuhen-sha, Sōsha of Suruga), and ceremonies continued at a corner of the shrine buildings. *Individual spirit invocation ceremonies for war dead were held in the three regions of Suruga, Izu, and Hamamatsu. *In the late 1880s, there was a growing movement to construct a single spirit invocation shrine for the entire prefecture. *November 13, 1899 (Meiji 32) - founded as a "Shared Spirit Invocation Shrine" with joint public and private support, in Kitabancho, Shizuoka City. *April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" by designation of the Ministry of the Interior. *October 8, 1942 (Shōwa 17) - Relocated to the current site due to the narrowness of the old site in central Shizuoka City. *March 28, 1946 (Shōwa 21) - established as a religious corporation under the Religious Corporations Act. *1947 (Shōwa 22) - Renamed "Shizutama Shrine" considering post-war conditions. *December 1952 (Shōwa 27) - Registered as a religious corporation with the approval of the prefectural governor. *1953 (Shōwa 28) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" and added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. *1978 (Shōwa 53) - New shrine office built due to the aging and narrowness of the old office. *October 22-23, 1998 (Heisei 10) - Centenary commemorative festival held. *October 22-23, 2018 (Heisei 30) - 120th anniversary commemorative festival held.
== Major festivals == [[File:Shizuoka ken Senbotsu Sensaishisha Ireihyo 19521103.jpg|thumb|200px|Memorial monument for the war dead and war victims of Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Designed by Tadashi Tanaka Architecture Office, constructed by Hirai Industry. 'Memorial Monument for the War Dead and War Victims of Shizuoka Prefecture', 1952.</ref>]] * Saitansai - January 1 * Genshisai - January 3 * Setsubunsai - February 3 * Kigensai - February 11 * Kinensai - February 17 * Tenchousai - February 23 * Shizuoka Prefecture Army War Dead Memorial Service - March 10 * Shōwasai - April 29 * Shōbu festival - May 5 * Shizuoka Prefecture Navy War Dead Memorial Service - May 27 * Otaue festival (at Shinsen-den) - June (auspicious day) * Nagoshi Oharae ceremony (Chinowa Kuguri) - June 30 * Tanabata festival - Early July (auspicious day) * Mantō Mitama festival - August 13-15 * Eirei Kenshōsai - August 13-15 * Nukihosai (at Shinsen-den) - October (auspicious day) * Harvest Thanksgiving festival - Same day as Nukihosai * Reiji Hoan ceremony (Gōshisai) - October 21 (when applicable) * festival "Irei Taisai" - October 22-23 * Meijisai - November 3 * Foundation Memorial festival - November 13 * Shichi-Go-San festival - November 15 * Niinamesai - November 23 * Oosusuharai festival - December 13 * Year-End Oharae ceremony - December 31 * New Year's Eve festival - December 31 * Tsukishizusai - Monthly on the 1st (except January, when Saitansai is held) * Tsukizusai - Monthly on the 13th (except November, when the Foundation Memorial festival is held) * Memorial Day ceremony - Several times monthly, and on memorial anniversaries as needed * Individual Unit Memorial Services - Held regularly (refer to official website)
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Gokoku Shrine|ja|護国神社}} * {{ill|Shizuoka Army Cemetery|ja|静岡陸軍墓地}} [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]]== External Links == * [https://shizuokagokoku.jp/ Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine (Official Site)] * [http://www.shizuoka-jinjacho.or.jp/shokai/jinja.php?id=4406003 Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine (Shizuoka Prefecture Shrine Office)] {{Shinto-navbox}} {{shinto-stub}} {{Normdaten}}

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:04 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine |image=[[File:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine.jpg|250px|Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]] |location=Shizuoka Prefecture, Shizuoka City, Aoi Ward, Yuzuki 366 |deity=76,233 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture |rank=Interior Minister Designated Gokoku Shrine, Beppyo Shrine |founded=1899 (Meiji 32) |main_hall=Nagare-zukuri |annual_festival={{ill|October 22|ja|10月22日}}, {{ill|October 23|ja|10月23日}} |latitude = 34.9875 |longitude = 138.4070 |ISO = JP-22 |label_position = bottom |map= Japan Shizuoka }} [[ja:靜岡縣護國神社]]
'''Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine''' (静岡縣護國神社[[ja:静岡縣護國神社]], Shizuoka-ken Gokoku Jinja) is a shrine located in {{ill|Shizuoka City|ja|静岡市}}, {{ill|Aoi Ward, Shizuoka|ja|葵区}}. The shrine crest is a circle with cherry blossoms. It enshrines over 76,000 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture from the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} to the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}.
During the war, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of the Interior (with prefectural rank). After the separation of state and shrine post-war, it was established and registered as a religious corporation under the newly formed {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. On May 29, 1953, it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines. The war relics hall on the second floor of the shrine office was relocated to the newly constructed Tsutsuji Hall, which also houses the Shizuoka Prefecture Bereaved Families Association Office, displaying about 1,000 war relics and portraits of the war dead.
== Hours == General worship hours: Shrine grounds are open from 6 AM to 5 PM (6 PM in spring and summer) * After closing hours, worship is allowed from a distance ≒ 24 hours operation (due to business profile limitations, this notation is used)
* Office hours (entry possible) are detailed on the official website == History == *1877 (Meiji 10) - A spirit invocation ceremony for war dead soldiers of the government forces was held at {{ill|Shizuoka Sengen Shrine|ja|静岡浅間神社}} (Kokuhen-sha, Sōsha of Suruga), and ceremonies continued at a corner of the shrine buildings. *Individual spirit invocation ceremonies for war dead were held in the three regions of Suruga, Izu, and Hamamatsu. *In the late 1880s, there was a growing movement to construct a single spirit invocation shrine for the entire prefecture. *November 13, 1899 (Meiji 32) - founded as a "Shared Spirit Invocation Shrine" with joint public and private support, in Kitabancho, Shizuoka City. *April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" by designation of the Ministry of the Interior. *October 8, 1942 (Shōwa 17) - Relocated to the current site due to the narrowness of the old site in central Shizuoka City. *March 28, 1946 (Shōwa 21) - established as a religious corporation under the Religious Corporations Act. *1947 (Shōwa 22) - Renamed "Shizutama Shrine" considering post-war conditions. *December 1952 (Shōwa 27) - Registered as a religious corporation with the approval of the prefectural governor. *1953 (Shōwa 28) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" and added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. *1978 (Shōwa 53) - New shrine office built due to the aging and narrowness of the old office. *October 22-23, 1998 (Heisei 10) - Centenary commemorative festival held. *October 22-23, 2018 (Heisei 30) - 120th anniversary commemorative festival held.
== Major festivals == [[File:Shizuoka ken Senbotsu Sensaishisha Ireihyo 19521103.jpg|thumb|200px|Memorial monument for the war dead and war victims of Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Designed by Tadashi Tanaka Architecture Office, constructed by Hirai Industry. 'Memorial Monument for the War Dead and War Victims of Shizuoka Prefecture', 1952.</ref>]] * Saitansai - January 1 * Genshisai - January 3 * Setsubunsai - February 3 * Kigensai - February 11 * Kinensai - February 17 * Tenchousai - February 23 * Shizuoka Prefecture Army War Dead Memorial Service - March 10 * Shōwasai - April 29 * Shōbu festival - May 5 * Shizuoka Prefecture Navy War Dead Memorial Service - May 27 * Otaue festival (at Shinsen-den) - June (auspicious day) * Nagoshi Oharae ceremony (Chinowa Kuguri) - June 30 * Tanabata festival - Early July (auspicious day) * Mantō Mitama festival - August 13-15 * Eirei Kenshōsai - August 13-15 * Nukihosai (at Shinsen-den) - October (auspicious day) * Harvest Thanksgiving festival - Same day as Nukihosai * Reiji Hoan ceremony (Gōshisai) - October 21 (when applicable) * festival "Irei Taisai" - October 22-23 * Meijisai - November 3 * Foundation Memorial festival - November 13 * Shichi-Go-San festival - November 15 * Niinamesai - November 23 * Oosusuharai festival - December 13 * Year-End Oharae ceremony - December 31 * New Year's Eve festival - December 31 * Tsukishizusai - Monthly on the 1st (except January, when Saitansai is held) * Tsukizusai - Monthly on the 13th (except November, when the Foundation Memorial festival is held) * Memorial Day ceremony - Several times monthly, and on memorial anniversaries as needed * Individual Unit Memorial Services - Held regularly (refer to official website)

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:04 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine |image=[[File:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine.jpg|250px|Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]] |location=Shizuoka Prefecture, Shizuoka City, Aoi Ward, Yuzuki 366 |deity=76,233 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture |rank=Interior Minister Designated Gokoku Shrine, Beppyo Shrine |founded=1899 (Meiji 32) |main_hall=Nagare-zukuri |annual_festival={{ill|October 22|ja|10月22日}}, {{ill|October 23|ja|10月23日}} |latitude = 34.9875 |longitude = 138.4070 |ISO = JP-22 |label_position = bottom |map= Japan Shizuoka }} [[ja:靜岡縣護國神社]]
'''Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine''' (静岡縣護國神社[[ja:静岡縣護國神社]], Shizuoka-ken Gokoku Jinja) is a shrine located in {{ill|Shizuoka City|ja|静岡市}}, {{ill|Aoi Ward, Shizuoka|ja|葵区}}. The shrine crest is a circle with cherry blossoms. It enshrines over 76,000 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture from the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} to the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}.
During the war, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of the Interior (with prefectural rank). After the separation of state and shrine post-war, it was established and registered as a religious corporation under the newly formed {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. On May 29, 1953, it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines. The war relics hall on the second floor of the shrine office was relocated to the newly constructed Tsutsuji Hall, which also houses the Shizuoka Prefecture Bereaved Families Association Office, displaying about 1,000 war relics and portraits of the war dead.
== Hours == General worship hours: Shrine grounds are open from 6 AM to 5 PM (6 PM in spring and summer) * After closing hours, worship is allowed from a distance ≒ 24 hours operation (due to business profile limitations, this notation is used)
* Office hours (entry possible) are detailed on the official website == History == *1877 (Meiji 10) - A spirit invocation ceremony for war dead soldiers of the government forces was held at {{ill|Shizuoka Sengen Shrine|ja|静岡浅間神社}} (Kokuhen-sha, Sōsha of Suruga), and ceremonies continued at a corner of the shrine buildings. *Individual spirit invocation ceremonies for war dead were held in the three regions of Suruga, Izu, and Hamamatsu. *In the late 1880s, there was a growing movement to construct a single spirit invocation shrine for the entire prefecture. *November 13, 1899 (Meiji 32) - founded as a "Shared Spirit Invocation Shrine" with joint public and private support, in Kitabancho, Shizuoka City. *April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" by designation of the Ministry of the Interior. *October 8, 1942 (Shōwa 17) - Relocated to the current site due to the narrowness of the old site in central Shizuoka City. *March 28, 1946 (Shōwa 21) - established as a religious corporation under the Religious Corporations Act. *1947 (Shōwa 22) - Renamed "Shizutama Shrine" considering post-war conditions. *December 1952 (Shōwa 27) - Registered as a religious corporation with the approval of the prefectural governor. *1953 (Shōwa 28) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" and added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. *1978 (Shōwa 53) - New shrine office built due to the aging and narrowness of the old office. *October 22-23, 1998 (Heisei 10) - Centenary commemorative festival held. *October 22-23, 2018 (Heisei 30) - 120th anniversary commemorative festival held.
== Major festivals == [[File:Shizuoka ken Senbotsu Sensaishisha Ireihyo 19521103.jpg|thumb|200px|Memorial monument for the war dead and war victims of Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Designed by Tadashi Tanaka Architecture Office, constructed by Hirai Industry. 'Memorial Monument for the War Dead and War Victims of Shizuoka Prefecture', 1952.</ref>]] * Saitansai - January 1 * Genshisai - January 3 * Setsubunsai - February 3 * Kigensai - February 11 * Kinensai - February 17 * Tenchousai - February 23 * Shizuoka Prefecture Army War Dead Memorial Service - March 10 * Shōwasai - April 29 * Shōbu festival - May 5 * Shizuoka Prefecture Navy War Dead Memorial Service - May 27 * Otaue festival (at Shinsen-den) - June (auspicious day) * Nagoshi Oharae ceremony (Chinowa Kuguri) - June 30 * Tanabata festival - Early July (auspicious day) * Mantō Mitama festival - August 13-15 * Eirei Kenshōsai - August 13-15 * Nukihosai (at Shinsen-den) - October (auspicious day) * Harvest Thanksgiving festival - Same day as Nukihosai * Reiji Hoan ceremony (Gōshisai) - October 21 (when applicable) * festival "Irei Taisai" - October 22-23 * Meijisai - November 3 * Foundation Memorial festival - November 13 * Shichi-Go-San festival - November 15 * Niinamesai - November 23 * Oosusuharai festival - December 13 * Year-End Oharae ceremony - December 31 * New Year's Eve festival - December 31 * Tsukishizusai - Monthly on the 1st (except January, when Saitansai is held) * Tsukizusai - Monthly on the 13th (except November, when the Foundation Memorial festival is held) * Memorial Day ceremony - Several times monthly, and on memorial anniversaries as needed * Individual Unit Memorial Services - Held regularly (refer to official website)

'Memorial Monument for the War Dead and War Victims of Shizuoka Prefecture', 1952.</ref>]] * Saitansai - January 1 * Genshisai - January 3 * Setsubunsai - February 3 * Kigensai - February 11 * Kinensai - February 17 * Tenchousai - February 23 * Shizuoka Prefecture Army War Dead Memorial Service - March 10 * Shōwasai - April 29 * Shōbu festival - May 5 * Shizuoka Prefecture Navy War Dead Memorial Service - May 27 * Otaue festival (at Shinsen-den) - June (auspicious day) * Nagoshi Oharae ceremony (Chinowa Kuguri) - June 30 * Tanabata festival - Early July (auspicious day) * Mantō Mitama festival - August 13-15 * Eirei Kenshōsai - August 13-15 * Nukihosai (at Shinsen-den) - October (auspicious day) * Harvest Thanksgiving festival - Same day as Nukihosai * Reiji Hoan ceremony (Gōshisai) - October 21 (when applicable) * festival "Irei Taisai" - October 22-23 * Meijisai - November 3 * Foundation Memorial festival - November 13 * Shichi-Go-San festival - November 15 * Niinamesai - November 23 * Oosusuharai festival - December 13 * Year-End Oharae ceremony - December 31 * New Year's Eve festival - December 31 * Tsukishizusai - Monthly on the 1st (except January, when Saitansai is held) * Tsukizusai - Monthly on the 13th (except November, when the Foundation Memorial festival is held) * Memorial Day ceremony - Several times monthly, and on memorial anniversaries as needed * Individual Unit Memorial Services - Held regularly (refer to official website)

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:04 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine |image=[[File:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine.jpg|250px|Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]] |location=Shizuoka Prefecture, Shizuoka City, Aoi Ward, Yuzuki 366 |deity=76,233 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture |rank=Interior Minister Designated Gokoku Shrine, Beppyo Shrine |founded=1899 (Meiji 32) |main_hall=Nagare-zukuri |annual_festival={{ill|October 22|ja|10月22日}}, {{ill|October 23|ja|10月23日}} |latitude = 34.9875 |longitude = 138.4070 |ISO = JP-22 |label_position = bottom |map= Japan Shizuoka }} [[ja:靜岡縣護國神社]]
'''Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine''' (静岡縣護國神社[[ja:静岡縣護國神社]], Shizuoka-ken Gokoku Jinja) is a shrine located in {{ill|Shizuoka City|ja|静岡市}}, {{ill|Aoi Ward, Shizuoka|ja|葵区}}. The shrine crest is a circle with cherry blossoms. It enshrines over 76,000 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture from the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} to the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}.
During the war, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of the Interior (with prefectural rank). After the separation of state and shrine post-war, it was established and registered as a religious corporation under the newly formed {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. On May 29, 1953, it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines. The war relics hall on the second floor of the shrine office was relocated to the newly constructed Tsutsuji Hall, which also houses the Shizuoka Prefecture Bereaved Families Association Office, displaying about 1,000 war relics and portraits of the war dead.
== Hours == General worship hours: Shrine grounds are open from 6 AM to 5 PM (6 PM in spring and summer) * After closing hours, worship is allowed from a distance ≒ 24 hours operation (due to business profile limitations, this notation is used)
* Office hours (entry possible) are detailed on the official website == History == *1877 (Meiji 10) - A spirit invocation ceremony for war dead soldiers of the government forces was held at {{ill|Shizuoka Sengen Shrine|ja|静岡浅間神社}} (Kokuhen-sha, Sōsha of Suruga), and ceremonies continued at a corner of the shrine buildings. *Individual spirit invocation ceremonies for war dead were held in the three regions of Suruga, Izu, and Hamamatsu. *In the late 1880s, there was a growing movement to construct a single spirit invocation shrine for the entire prefecture. *November 13, 1899 (Meiji 32) - founded as a "Shared Spirit Invocation Shrine" with joint public and private support, in Kitabancho, Shizuoka City. *April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" by designation of the Ministry of the Interior. *October 8, 1942 (Shōwa 17) - Relocated to the current site due to the narrowness of the old site in central Shizuoka City. *March 28, 1946 (Shōwa 21) - established as a religious corporation under the Religious Corporations Act. *1947 (Shōwa 22) - Renamed "Shizutama Shrine" considering post-war conditions. *December 1952 (Shōwa 27) - Registered as a religious corporation with the approval of the prefectural governor. *1953 (Shōwa 28) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" and added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. *1978 (Shōwa 53) - New shrine office built due to the aging and narrowness of the old office. *October 22-23, 1998 (Heisei 10) - Centenary commemorative festival held. *October 22-23, 2018 (Heisei 30) - 120th anniversary commemorative festival held.
== Major festivals == [[File:Shizuoka ken Senbotsu Sensaishisha Ireihyo 19521103.jpg|thumb|200px|Memorial monument for the war dead and war victims of Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Designed by Tadashi Tanaka Architecture Office, constructed by Hirai Industry. * Office hours (entry possible) are detailed on the official website == History == *1877 (Meiji 10) - A spirit invocation ceremony for war dead soldiers of the government forces was held at {{ill|Shizuoka Sengen Shrine|ja|静岡浅間神社}} (Kokuhen-sha, Sōsha of Suruga), and ceremonies continued at a corner of the shrine buildings. *Individual spirit invocation ceremonies for war dead were held in the three regions of Suruga, Izu, and Hamamatsu. *In the late 1880s, there was a growing movement to construct a single spirit invocation shrine for the entire prefecture. *November 13, 1899 (Meiji 32) - founded as a "Shared Spirit Invocation Shrine" with joint public and private support, in Kitabancho, Shizuoka City. *April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" by designation of the Ministry of the Interior. *October 8, 1942 (Shōwa 17) - Relocated to the current site due to the narrowness of the old site in central Shizuoka City. *March 28, 1946 (Shōwa 21) - established as a religious corporation under the Religious Corporations Act. *1947 (Shōwa 22) - Renamed "Shizutama Shrine" considering post-war conditions. *December 1952 (Shōwa 27) - Registered as a religious corporation with the approval of the prefectural governor. *1953 (Shōwa 28) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" and added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. *1978 (Shōwa 53) - New shrine office built due to the aging and narrowness of the old office. *October 22-23, 1998 (Heisei 10) - Centenary commemorative festival held. *October 22-23, 2018 (Heisei 30) - 120th anniversary commemorative festival held.
== Major festivals == [[File:Shizuoka ken Senbotsu Sensaishisha Ireihyo 19521103.jpg|thumb|200px|Memorial monument for the war dead and war victims of Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Designed by Tadashi Tanaka Architecture Office, constructed by Hirai Industry. 'Memorial Monument for the War Dead and War Victims of Shizuoka Prefecture', 1952.</ref>]] * Saitansai - January 1 * Genshisai - January 3 * Setsubunsai - February 3 * Kigensai - February 11 * Kinensai - February 17 * Tenchousai - February 23 * Shizuoka Prefecture Army War Dead Memorial Service - March 10 * Shōwasai - April 29 * Shōbu festival - May 5 * Shizuoka Prefecture Navy War Dead Memorial Service - May 27 * Otaue festival (at Shinsen-den) - June (auspicious day) * Nagoshi Oharae ceremony (Chinowa Kuguri) - June 30 * Tanabata festival - Early July (auspicious day) * Mantō Mitama festival - August 13-15 * Eirei Kenshōsai - August 13-15 * Nukihosai (at Shinsen-den) - October (auspicious day) * Harvest Thanksgiving festival - Same day as Nukihosai * Reiji Hoan ceremony (Gōshisai) - October 21 (when applicable) * festival "Irei Taisai" - October 22-23 * Meijisai - November 3 * Foundation Memorial festival - November 13 * Shichi-Go-San festival - November 15 * Niinamesai - November 23 * Oosusuharai festival - December 13 * Year-End Oharae ceremony - December 31 * New Year's Eve festival - December 31 * Tsukishizusai - Monthly on the 1st (except January, when Saitansai is held) * Tsukizusai - Monthly on the 13th (except November, when the Foundation Memorial festival is held) * Memorial Day ceremony - Several times monthly, and on memorial anniversaries as needed * Individual Unit Memorial Services - Held regularly (refer to official website)

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:04 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine |image=[[File:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine.jpg|250px|Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]] |location=Shizuoka Prefecture, Shizuoka City, Aoi Ward, Yuzuki 366 |deity=76,233 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture |rank=Interior Minister Designated Gokoku Shrine, Beppyo Shrine |founded=1899 (Meiji 32) |main_hall=Nagare-zukuri |annual_festival={{ill|October 22|ja|10月22日}}, {{ill|October 23|ja|10月23日}} |latitude = 34.9875 |longitude = 138.4070 |ISO = JP-22 |label_position = bottom |map= Japan Shizuoka }} [[ja:靜岡縣護國神社]]
'''Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine''' (静岡縣護國神社[[ja:静岡縣護國神社]], Shizuoka-ken Gokoku Jinja) is a shrine located in {{ill|Shizuoka City|ja|静岡市}}, {{ill|Aoi Ward, Shizuoka|ja|葵区}}. The shrine crest is a circle with cherry blossoms. It enshrines over 76,000 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture from the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} to the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}.
During the war, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of the Interior (with prefectural rank). After the separation of state and shrine post-war, it was established and registered as a religious corporation under the newly formed {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. On May 29, 1953, it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines. The war relics hall on the second floor of the shrine office was relocated to the newly constructed Tsutsuji Hall, which also houses the Shizuoka Prefecture Bereaved Families Association Office, displaying about 1,000 war relics and portraits of the war dead.
== Hours == General worship hours: Shrine grounds are open from 6 AM to 5 PM (6 PM in spring and summer) * After closing hours, worship is allowed from a distance ≒ 24 hours operation (due to business profile limitations, this notation is used)
* Office hours (entry possible) are detailed on the official website == History == *1877 (Meiji 10) - A spirit invocation ceremony for war dead soldiers of the government forces was held at {{ill|Shizuoka Sengen Shrine|ja|静岡浅間神社}} (Kokuhen-sha, Sōsha of Suruga), and ceremonies continued at a corner of the shrine buildings. *Individual spirit invocation ceremonies for war dead were held in the three regions of Suruga, Izu, and Hamamatsu. *In the late 1880s, there was a growing movement to construct a single spirit invocation shrine for the entire prefecture. *November 13, 1899 (Meiji 32) - founded as a "Shared Spirit Invocation Shrine" with joint public and private support, in Kitabancho, Shizuoka City. *April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" by designation of the Ministry of the Interior. *October 8, 1942 (Shōwa 17) - Relocated to the current site due to the narrowness of the old site in central Shizuoka City. *March 28, 1946 (Shōwa 21) - established as a religious corporation under the Religious Corporations Act. *1947 (Shōwa 22) - Renamed "Shizutama Shrine" considering post-war conditions. *December 1952 (Shōwa 27) - Registered as a religious corporation with the approval of the prefectural governor. *1953 (Shōwa 28) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" and added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. *1978 (Shōwa 53) - New shrine office built due to the aging and narrowness of the old office. *October 22-23, 1998 (Heisei 10) - Centenary commemorative festival held. *October 22-23, 2018 (Heisei 30) - 120th anniversary commemorative festival held.
== Major festivals == [[File:Shizuoka ken Senbotsu Sensaishisha Ireihyo 19521103.jpg|thumb|200px|Memorial monument for the war dead and war victims of Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Designed by Tadashi Tanaka Architecture Office, constructed by Hirai Industry. The war relics hall on the second floor of the shrine office was relocated to the newly constructed Tsutsuji Hall, which also houses the Shizuoka Prefecture Bereaved Families Association Office, displaying about 1,000 war relics and portraits of the war dead.
== Hours == General worship hours: Shrine grounds are open from 6 AM to 5 PM (6 PM in spring and summer) * After closing hours, worship is allowed from a distance ≒ 24 hours operation (due to business profile limitations, this notation is used)
* Office hours (entry possible) are detailed on the official website == History == *1877 (Meiji 10) - A spirit invocation ceremony for war dead soldiers of the government forces was held at {{ill|Shizuoka Sengen Shrine|ja|静岡浅間神社}} (Kokuhen-sha, Sōsha of Suruga), and ceremonies continued at a corner of the shrine buildings. *Individual spirit invocation ceremonies for war dead were held in the three regions of Suruga, Izu, and Hamamatsu. *In the late 1880s, there was a growing movement to construct a single spirit invocation shrine for the entire prefecture. *November 13, 1899 (Meiji 32) - founded as a "Shared Spirit Invocation Shrine" with joint public and private support, in Kitabancho, Shizuoka City. *April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" by designation of the Ministry of the Interior. *October 8, 1942 (Shōwa 17) - Relocated to the current site due to the narrowness of the old site in central Shizuoka City. *March 28, 1946 (Shōwa 21) - established as a religious corporation under the Religious Corporations Act. *1947 (Shōwa 22) - Renamed "Shizutama Shrine" considering post-war conditions. *December 1952 (Shōwa 27) - Registered as a religious corporation with the approval of the prefectural governor. *1953 (Shōwa 28) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" and added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. *1978 (Shōwa 53) - New shrine office built due to the aging and narrowness of the old office. *October 22-23, 1998 (Heisei 10) - Centenary commemorative festival held. *October 22-23, 2018 (Heisei 30) - 120th anniversary commemorative festival held.
== Major festivals == [[File:Shizuoka ken Senbotsu Sensaishisha Ireihyo 19521103.jpg|thumb|200px|Memorial monument for the war dead and war victims of Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Designed by Tadashi Tanaka Architecture Office, constructed by Hirai Industry. 'Memorial Monument for the War Dead and War Victims of Shizuoka Prefecture', 1952.</ref>]] * Saitansai - January 1 * Genshisai - January 3 * Setsubunsai - February 3 * Kigensai - February 11 * Kinensai - February 17 * Tenchousai - February 23 * Shizuoka Prefecture Army War Dead Memorial Service - March 10 * Shōwasai - April 29 * Shōbu festival - May 5 * Shizuoka Prefecture Navy War Dead Memorial Service - May 27 * Otaue festival (at Shinsen-den) - June (auspicious day) * Nagoshi Oharae ceremony (Chinowa Kuguri) - June 30 * Tanabata festival - Early July (auspicious day) * Mantō Mitama festival - August 13-15 * Eirei Kenshōsai - August 13-15 * Nukihosai (at Shinsen-den) - October (auspicious day) * Harvest Thanksgiving festival - Same day as Nukihosai * Reiji Hoan ceremony (Gōshisai) - October 21 (when applicable) * festival "Irei Taisai" - October 22-23 * Meijisai - November 3 * Foundation Memorial festival - November 13 * Shichi-Go-San festival - November 15 * Niinamesai - November 23 * Oosusuharai festival - December 13 * Year-End Oharae ceremony - December 31 * New Year's Eve festival - December 31 * Tsukishizusai - Monthly on the 1st (except January, when Saitansai is held) * Tsukizusai - Monthly on the 13th (except November, when the Foundation Memorial festival is held) * Memorial Day ceremony - Several times monthly, and on memorial anniversaries as needed * Individual Unit Memorial Services - Held regularly (refer to official website)

'''Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine''' (静岡縣護國神社[[ja:静岡縣護國神社]], Shizuoka-ken Gokoku Jinja) is a shrine located in {{ill|Shizuoka City|ja|静岡市}}, {{ill|Aoi Ward, Shizuoka|ja|葵区}}. The shrine crest is a circle with cherry blossoms. It enshrines over 76,000 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture from the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} to the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}.
During the war, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of the Interior (with prefectural rank). After the separation of state and shrine post-war, it was established and registered as a religious corporation under the newly formed {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. On May 29, 1953, it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines. The war relics hall on the second floor of the shrine office was relocated to the newly constructed Tsutsuji Hall, which also houses the Shizuoka Prefecture Bereaved Families Association Office, displaying about 1,000 war relics and portraits of the war dead.
== Hours == General worship hours: Shrine grounds are open from 6 AM to 5 PM (6 PM in spring and summer) * After closing hours, worship is allowed from a distance ≒ 24 hours operation (due to business profile limitations, this notation is used)

* Office hours (entry possible) are detailed on the official website == History == *1877 (Meiji 10) - A spirit invocation ceremony for war dead soldiers of the government forces was held at {{ill|Shizuoka Sengen Shrine|ja|静岡浅間神社}} (Kokuhen-sha, Sōsha of Suruga), and ceremonies continued at a corner of the shrine buildings. *Individual spirit invocation ceremonies for war dead were held in the three regions of Suruga, Izu, and Hamamatsu. *In the late 1880s, there was a growing movement to construct a single spirit invocation shrine for the entire prefecture. *November 13, 1899 (Meiji 32) - founded as a "Shared Spirit Invocation Shrine" with joint public and private support, in Kitabancho, Shizuoka City. *April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" by designation of the Ministry of the Interior. *October 8, 1942 (Shōwa 17) - Relocated to the current site due to the narrowness of the old site in central Shizuoka City. *March 28, 1946 (Shōwa 21) - established as a religious corporation under the Religious Corporations Act. *1947 (Shōwa 22) - Renamed "Shizutama Shrine" considering post-war conditions. *December 1952 (Shōwa 27) - Registered as a religious corporation with the approval of the prefectural governor. *1953 (Shōwa 28) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" and added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. *1978 (Shōwa 53) - New shrine office built due to the aging and narrowness of the old office. *October 22-23, 1998 (Heisei 10) - Centenary commemorative festival held. *October 22-23, 2018 (Heisei 30) - 120th anniversary commemorative festival held.

'Memorial Monument for the War Dead and War Victims of Shizuoka Prefecture', 1952.</ref>]] * Saitansai - January 1 * Genshisai - January 3 * Setsubunsai - February 3 * Kigensai - February 11 * Kinensai - February 17 * Tenchousai - February 23 * Shizuoka Prefecture Army War Dead Memorial Service - March 10 * Shōwasai - April 29 * Shōbu festival - May 5 * Shizuoka Prefecture Navy War Dead Memorial Service - May 27 * Otaue festival (at Shinsen-den) - June (auspicious day) * Nagoshi Oharae ceremony (Chinowa Kuguri) - June 30 * Tanabata festival - Early July (auspicious day) * Mantō Mitama festival - August 13-15 * Eirei Kenshōsai - August 13-15 * Nukihosai (at Shinsen-den) - October (auspicious day) * Harvest Thanksgiving festival - Same day as Nukihosai * Reiji Hoan ceremony (Gōshisai) - October 21 (when applicable) * festival "Irei Taisai" - October 22-23 * Meijisai - November 3 * Foundation Memorial festival - November 13 * Shichi-Go-San festival - November 15 * Niinamesai - November 23 * Oosusuharai festival - December 13 * Year-End Oharae ceremony - December 31 * New Year's Eve festival - December 31 * Tsukishizusai - Monthly on the 1st (except January, when Saitansai is held) * Tsukizusai - Monthly on the 13th (except November, when the Foundation Memorial festival is held) * Memorial Day ceremony - Several times monthly, and on memorial anniversaries as needed * Individual Unit Memorial Services - Held regularly (refer to official website)

'''Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine''' (静岡縣護國神社[[ja:静岡縣護國神社]], Shizuoka-ken Gokoku Jinja) is a shrine located in {{ill|Shizuoka City|ja|静岡市}}, {{ill|Aoi Ward, Shizuoka|ja|葵区}}. The shrine crest is a circle with cherry blossoms. It enshrines over 76,000 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture from the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} to the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}.
During the war, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of the Interior (with prefectural rank). After the separation of state and shrine post-war, it was established and registered as a religious corporation under the newly formed {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. On May 29, 1953, it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines. The war relics hall on the second floor of the shrine office was relocated to the newly constructed Tsutsuji Hall, which also houses the Shizuoka Prefecture Bereaved Families Association Office, displaying about 1,000 war relics and portraits of the war dead.
== Hours == General worship hours: Shrine grounds are open from 6 AM to 5 PM (6 PM in spring and summer) * After closing hours, worship is allowed from a distance ≒ 24 hours operation (due to business profile limitations, this notation is used)
* Office hours (entry possible) are detailed on the official website == History == *1877 (Meiji 10) - A spirit invocation ceremony for war dead soldiers of the government forces was held at {{ill|Shizuoka Sengen Shrine|ja|静岡浅間神社}} (Kokuhen-sha, Sōsha of Suruga), and ceremonies continued at a corner of the shrine buildings. *Individual spirit invocation ceremonies for war dead were held in the three regions of Suruga, Izu, and Hamamatsu. *In the late 1880s, there was a growing movement to construct a single spirit invocation shrine for the entire prefecture. *November 13, 1899 (Meiji 32) - founded as a "Shared Spirit Invocation Shrine" with joint public and private support, in Kitabancho, Shizuoka City. *April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" by designation of the Ministry of the Interior. *October 8, 1942 (Shōwa 17) - Relocated to the current site due to the narrowness of the old site in central Shizuoka City. *March 28, 1946 (Shōwa 21) - established as a religious corporation under the Religious Corporations Act. *1947 (Shōwa 22) - Renamed "Shizutama Shrine" considering post-war conditions. *December 1952 (Shōwa 27) - Registered as a religious corporation with the approval of the prefectural governor. *1953 (Shōwa 28) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" and added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. *1978 (Shōwa 53) - New shrine office built due to the aging and narrowness of the old office. *October 22-23, 1998 (Heisei 10) - Centenary commemorative festival held. *October 22-23, 2018 (Heisei 30) - 120th anniversary commemorative festival held.
== Major festivals == [[File:Shizuoka ken Senbotsu Sensaishisha Ireihyo 19521103.jpg|thumb|200px|Memorial monument for the war dead and war victims of Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Designed by Tadashi Tanaka Architecture Office, constructed by Hirai Industry. == Hours == General worship hours: Shrine grounds are open from 6 AM to 5 PM (6 PM in spring and summer) * After closing hours, worship is allowed from a distance ≒ 24 hours operation (due to business profile limitations, this notation is used)
* Office hours (entry possible) are detailed on the official website == History == *1877 (Meiji 10) - A spirit invocation ceremony for war dead soldiers of the government forces was held at {{ill|Shizuoka Sengen Shrine|ja|静岡浅間神社}} (Kokuhen-sha, Sōsha of Suruga), and ceremonies continued at a corner of the shrine buildings. *Individual spirit invocation ceremonies for war dead were held in the three regions of Suruga, Izu, and Hamamatsu. *In the late 1880s, there was a growing movement to construct a single spirit invocation shrine for the entire prefecture. *November 13, 1899 (Meiji 32) - founded as a "Shared Spirit Invocation Shrine" with joint public and private support, in Kitabancho, Shizuoka City. *April 1, 1939 (Shōwa 14) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" by designation of the Ministry of the Interior. *October 8, 1942 (Shōwa 17) - Relocated to the current site due to the narrowness of the old site in central Shizuoka City. *March 28, 1946 (Shōwa 21) - established as a religious corporation under the Religious Corporations Act. *1947 (Shōwa 22) - Renamed "Shizutama Shrine" considering post-war conditions. *December 1952 (Shōwa 27) - Registered as a religious corporation with the approval of the prefectural governor. *1953 (Shōwa 28) - Renamed "Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine" and added to the list of Beppyo Shrines under the Association of Shinto Shrines. *1978 (Shōwa 53) - New shrine office built due to the aging and narrowness of the old office. *October 22-23, 1998 (Heisei 10) - Centenary commemorative festival held. *October 22-23, 2018 (Heisei 30) - 120th anniversary commemorative festival held.
== Major festivals == [[File:Shizuoka ken Senbotsu Sensaishisha Ireihyo 19521103.jpg|thumb|200px|Memorial monument for the war dead and war victims of Shizuoka Prefecture<ref>Designed by Tadashi Tanaka Architecture Office, constructed by Hirai Industry. 'Memorial Monument for the War Dead and War Victims of Shizuoka Prefecture', 1952.</ref>]] * Saitansai - January 1 * Genshisai - January 3 * Setsubunsai - February 3 * Kigensai - February 11 * Kinensai - February 17 * Tenchousai - February 23 * Shizuoka Prefecture Army War Dead Memorial Service - March 10 * Shōwasai - April 29 * Shōbu festival - May 5 * Shizuoka Prefecture Navy War Dead Memorial Service - May 27 * Otaue festival (at Shinsen-den) - June (auspicious day) * Nagoshi Oharae ceremony (Chinowa Kuguri) - June 30 * Tanabata festival - Early July (auspicious day) * Mantō Mitama festival - August 13-15 * Eirei Kenshōsai - August 13-15 * Nukihosai (at Shinsen-den) - October (auspicious day) * Harvest Thanksgiving festival - Same day as Nukihosai * Reiji Hoan ceremony (Gōshisai) - October 21 (when applicable) * festival "Irei Taisai" - October 22-23 * Meijisai - November 3 * Foundation Memorial festival - November 13 * Shichi-Go-San festival - November 15 * Niinamesai - November 23 * Oosusuharai festival - December 13 * Year-End Oharae ceremony - December 31 * New Year's Eve festival - December 31 * Tsukishizusai - Monthly on the 1st (except January, when Saitansai is held) * Tsukizusai - Monthly on the 13th (except November, when the Foundation Memorial festival is held) * Memorial Day ceremony - Several times monthly, and on memorial anniversaries as needed * Individual Unit Memorial Services - Held regularly (refer to official website)

{{出典の明記|date=2017年8月28日 (月) 09:04 (UTC)}} {{Infobox religious building|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]|religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]]| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |name=Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine |image=[[File:Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine.jpg|250px|Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine]] |location=Shizuoka Prefecture, Shizuoka City, Aoi Ward, Yuzuki 366 |deity=76,233 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture |rank=Interior Minister Designated Gokoku Shrine, Beppyo Shrine |founded=1899 (Meiji 32) |main_hall=Nagare-zukuri |annual_festival={{ill|October 22|ja|10月22日}}, {{ill|October 23|ja|10月23日}} |latitude = 34.9875 |longitude = 138.4070 |ISO = JP-22 |label_position = bottom |map= Japan Shizuoka }} [[ja:靜岡縣護國神社]]
'''Shizuoka Gokoku Shrine''' (静岡縣護國神社[[ja:静岡縣護國神社]], Shizuoka-ken Gokoku Jinja) is a shrine located in {{ill|Shizuoka City|ja|静岡市}}, {{ill|Aoi Ward, Shizuoka|ja|葵区}}. The shrine crest is a circle with cherry blossoms. It enshrines over 76,000 war dead and martyrs from Shizuoka Prefecture from the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}} to the {{ill|Pacific War|ja|太平洋戦争}}.
During the war, it was designated as a Gokoku Shrine by the Ministry of the Interior (with prefectural rank). After the separation of state and shrine post-war, it was established and registered as a religious corporation under the newly formed {{ill|Association of Shinto Shrines|ja|神社本庁}}. On May 29, 1953, it was added to the list of Beppyo Shrines. The war relics hall on the second floor of the shrine office was relocated to the newly constructed Tsutsuji Hall, which also houses the Shizuoka Prefecture Bereaved Families Association Office, displaying about 1,000 war relics and portraits of the war dead.
== Hours == General worship hours: Shrine grounds are open from 6 AM to 5 PM (6 PM in spring and summer) * After closing hours, worship is allowed from a distance ≒ 24 hours operation (due to business profile limitations, this notation is used)
